smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
hi Edward, just went through your site, not sure how H2Ocean isn't already on my client list haha.

that's not really why I'm here though. I do Meta ads for ecom brands, and rather than pitch you some BS, I just went ahead and built you a few.

built three ad mockups from H2Ocean's actual products, plus a word-class brand playbook I put together from your site and reviews. it's finished and ready to go on my end.

want me to send it your way?

- Kyle

Kyle 

 Sure send me what you are presenting for Meta Ads, we are interested in the creative content.
 

 Thanks
 Eddie

here you go Edward - 2 ad mockups built from H2Ocean's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
Just set us up for tomorrow. 

 Thanks
 Eddie

copy.tldr: H2Ocean is a 20-year-old natural sea salt mouthwash brand with strong healing claims but no ads running and no visible reviews, so this call is about starting clean and proving one healing-focused ad can work before we scale.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A natural oral care brand based in Stuart, Florida. They have sold a patented sea salt mouthwash for over 20 years. This is the oral care side of the larger H2Ocean name, which people may know from tattoo and piercing aftercare.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Two products, both the same Healing Rinse mouthwash in different flavors: Arctic Ocean Mint and Fresh Lemon Ice. It is made with Red Sea salt, xylitol, and a natural enzyme. No alcohol, no fluoride, no sugar, no artificial flavors. Note the site lists it at $11 in one place and $17 on the mint product page, and the Lemon Ice is currently sold out. Confirm real prices on the call.
deep_dive.positioning: A premium, natural alternative to harsh alcohol-based mouthwashes. They lean hard on healing and recovery: post-dental work, oral piercings, canker sores, sore throats, dry mouth, and chemo mouth. The hook is that it soothes and does not burn.
deep_dive.who_buys: Health-conscious adults who need gentle relief. People recovering from dental work or oral surgery. People with fresh piercings. People with canker sores or sore throats. Families who want a non-burning rinse safe for all ages. Note: dentists and dental pros are also an audience they speak to.
customer_voice: This is great for post-dental work and for oral piercings! It was the only thing that helped my tongue piercing heal. Two thumbs up
strengths: A real, specific pain the product solves. People in pain from piercings, canker sores, or dental work search for relief and buy fast. That is a strong ad angle.
20 years in business, a patent, and a clear point of difference (sea salt, no alcohol, does not burn). Easy to make believable ads from.
gaps: No reviews visible anywhere on the site, so there is no social proof to build ads on. Ads need trust and right now the site shows none.

No ads are running today. We are starting from zero, which means the first 30 days is about learning, not scaling. Also a price mismatch on the site ($11 vs $17) and one flavor sold out, which can hurt a paid campaign.

game_plan.headline: Lead with the healing and recovery angle on the Arctic Ocean Mint, prove one ad can sell at a small budget, then scale the winner and add the second flavor.

phases.goal: Prove one ad can bring in sales at a small, safe budget. We are learning what works, not scaling.
campaigns: One sales campaign with 2 to 3 audiences kept broad, since we are starting cold and want Meta to find buyers.
A simple retargeting campaign to catch people who visit the site but do not buy.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint. We lead with this one because the Lemon Ice is currently sold out, and mint is the safe, familiar flavor most first-time mouthwash buyers reach for. Confirm mint is the bestseller on the call.
phases.creative: Start with the healing and recovery ad from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet, the one built around the piercing and post-dental angle. It goes live first because it matches the strongest real pain and the one real quote we have (the tongue piercing review). Run the other sheet ad as the second test. Keep copy inside the playbook rules: no 'cures', no 'treats chemo', no 'eco-friendly' claims.
phases.budget: Around $100 to $150 a day to start.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One ad clearly beats the others and brings in sales at a cost we are happy to keep paying. We now know which pain and which flavor to push.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning ad and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad from month one, raising budget in steps.
Add 2 to 3 new versions of the winning angle to keep it fresh.
Widen audiences and add lookalike audiences built from month-one buyers.
lead_products: Still Arctic Ocean Mint as the lead, unless month-one data or restocked Lemon Ice tells us otherwise.
phases.creative: Take the winning angle and make more ads like it. If piercings won, make more piercing ads. If post-dental won, lean there. Add real customer reviews to the ads as soon as we can collect them.
phases.budget: Step up toward $250 to $350 a day only if month one hit its target.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Sales grow while the cost to get a sale stays steady. The winning angle holds up at higher spend.

phases.goal: Scale the winner and bring in the second product.
campaigns: Launch the Fresh Lemon Ice flavor as a second product, once it is back in stock.
Test one new audience or need (for example dry mouth or sore throat) to open a new group of buyers.
lead_products: Fresh Lemon Ice added as the second product. Only launch it once stock is confirmed, or the ads will send people to a sold-out page.
phases.creative: Best performing ads keep running. New ads for the second flavor. Bring in whatever new reviews we have gathered to build more trust.
phases.budget: Scale in steps based on results, not a fixed number.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products selling, a steady cost per sale, and a repeatable set of ads we can keep spending behind.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get roughly 30 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience works

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We would set a target together after we know their real price and margin
estimates.basis: the site shows $11 in the catalog and $17 on the mint page, so we cannot pin an order value yet. Once Eddie confirms the real average order value and margin, we set an honest 90-day target. Guessing now would be dishonest.

assumptions: The real selling price and average order value are close to what the site shows. If it is nearer $11 than $17, the margin may be too thin for paid ads and the plan changes.
They can restock Fresh Lemon Ice before month three. If not, we stay on mint only.
They can start collecting and showing customer reviews, since ads need social proof to work.
There is inventory to support scaling. No point growing ads if they sell out.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: We found no ads running. If that is true, we start from zero. If they run ads somewhere else, ask what and how it is going.

questions.question: Who handles marketing for you today, in-house or an agency?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is a team, a past agency, or nobody. Tells us who we work with and who to keep happy.

questions.question: Have you tried Meta ads before, and if so what happened?
questions.listening_for: Past pain. If they tried and it failed, find out why (bad creative, low budget, wrong product) so we do not repeat it.

questions.question: I see $11 in one spot and $17 in another on the site. What is the real selling price, and roughly what do you keep after making and shipping it?
questions.listening_for: The real price and margin. At $11 the math for paid ads is tight. We need a healthy margin to afford to buy a customer.

questions.question: What does an average order look like? Do people usually buy one bottle or stock up?
questions.listening_for: Order value. If people buy the 64oz size or bundle, the math works far better than a single $11 bottle.

questions.question: When someone lands on your product page, roughly how often do they buy?
questions.listening_for: Whether the site converts. If traffic already turns into sales, ads are the lever. If not, we fix the page first or we waste ad money.

questions.question: I did not see any reviews on the site. Do you collect them, and can we get them showing on the page?
questions.listening_for: This is the big one. No social proof means weaker ads and a weaker page. We need reviews live before we spend much.

questions.question: Which flavor and which bottle size sells best?
questions.listening_for: Confirms mint is the lead. Tells us what to feature and whether to push the bigger sizes for a better order value.

questions.question: Is Fresh Lemon Ice coming back in stock, and when?
questions.listening_for: It is sold out now. We cannot advertise a product people cannot buy. This sets the timing for phase three.

questions.question: Of all the uses (piercings, dental work, canker sores, dry mouth, chemo), which brings you the most buyers?
questions.listening_for: The strongest pain to lead ads with. Note we must avoid medical 'cure' and chemo-treatment claims in the copy.

questions.question: Do you get much traffic from Google searches, and are you running any Google ads?
questions.listening_for: People clearly search for things like 'mouthwash for canker sores' and 'piercing aftercare rinse'. That is a real opening for Google search ads and SEO later, once Meta is working.

questions.question: You have a big Knowledge Center with clinical studies and articles. Is that bringing in any visitors from search?
questions.listening_for: They already made content. If it is not ranking, there is honest SEO work to do. If it is, we build on it.

objections.objection: We have never run ads and we are not sure they work for us.
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start small. The first 30 days is a test at $100 to $150 a day, not a big bet. We prove one ad can sell before we spend real money. If nothing works, you have lost very little and learned a lot.

objections.objection: Our mouthwash is only about $11. Can ads even pay off at that price?
objections.answer: Fair question, and it is the first thing we need to check. If it is closer to $17 or people buy the bigger sizes and stock up, the math works. If most orders are a single $11 bottle, we would focus on bundles and repeat buyers first. I will not run ads that lose you money.

objections.objection: We just want the creative, not a full service.
objections.answer: Understood, the ads are yours either way. But an ad only works when the audience, budget, and the page behind it are set up right. The call is 30 minutes to show how I would actually run these, then you decide. No pressure.

objections.objection: We have been around 20 years and do fine without ads.
objections.answer: That is a real strength, most brands cannot say that. But you have no ads running and no reviews showing, which means new customers online never find you or trust you. Paid ads are how you reach the people already in pain and searching for exactly what you make.

red_flags.flag: No reviews visible anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Ads need trust to work. With no social proof on the page or in the ads, people click but do not buy. We should get reviews live before spending much.

red_flags.flag: Price mismatch on the site: $11 in the catalog, $17 on the mint product page.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot set an honest order value or revenue target until we know the real price. It also looks sloppy to a buyer landing on the page.

red_flags.flag: Fresh Lemon Ice is sold out.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We must not advertise a product people cannot buy. This forces mint as the lead and pushes the second product to phase three.

red_flags.flag: Low unit price if it really is $11.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A single cheap bottle may not carry the cost of buying a customer through ads. If so, we lean on bundles, bigger sizes, and repeat buyers, or the plan changes.

red_flags.flag: Health and medical claims on the site (chemo mouth, healing, canker sores).
red_flags.why_it_matters: Meta restricts health claims and the playbook bans 'cures' and chemo-treatment wording. Ad copy must stay careful or ads get rejected or accounts flagged.

other_channel_openings: Google search ads: people actively search for relief terms like 'mouthwash for canker sores', 'piercing aftercare rinse', and 'alcohol-free mouthwash'. That is real buying intent we can catch once Meta is working.
SEO: they already have a Knowledge Center with articles and clinical studies. If it is not bringing in search traffic, there is honest work to help those pages rank for the pains people search.
